solution.answer: $H_0 \cong H_4 \cong \mathbb{Z}$;\quad $H_2 \cong \mathbb{Z}^2$;\quad $H_1 = H_3 = 0$. Cup product: $a_1^2 = a_2^2 = [\text{pt}]$, $a_1 \cup a_2 = 0$, where $a_1, a_2$ are generators of each $H^2(\mathbb{C}P^2)$.
steps: Write $X = \mathbb{C}P^2 \# \mathbb{C}P^2 = U \cup V$ where $U = \mathbb{C}P^2 \setminus \{D^4\}$, $V = \mathbb{C}P^2 \setminus \{D^4\}$, and $U \cap V \simeq S^3$.
$H_k(\mathbb{C}P^2 \setminus D^4)$: removing an open disk kills $H_4$ and $H_3$ but preserves $H_0 = \mathbb{Z}$, $H_2 = \mathbb{Z}$. (Use the long exact sequence of the pair $(\mathbb{C}P^2, D^4)$.)
Mayer–Vietoris for $X = U \cup V$, $U \cap V \simeq S^3$: in degrees 2 and below, the sequence gives $H_2(X) = H_2(U) \oplus H_2(V) = \mathbb{Z}^2$.
Since $H_1(U) = H_1(V) = H_1(S^3) = 0$, we get $H_1(X) = 0$. Similarly $H_3(X) = 0$. $H_4(X) = \mathbb{Z}$ (orientable closed 4-manifold).

Cup product: Let $a_1, a_2 \in H^2(X)$ be the generators (pulled back from each $\mathbb{C}P^2$ factor). Since $a_i$ is supported on $U$ (or $V$) and $a_j$ on $V$ (or $U$), and $U \cap V = S^3$ has $H^2 = 0$, we get $a_1 \cup a_2 = 0$.

But $a_i \cup a_i$: this equals the generator of $H^4$ coming from the $i$-th copy of $\mathbb{C}P^2$. The intersection form is $\begin{pmatrix}1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1\end{pmatrix}$, i.e.\ $\langle 1 \rangle \oplus \langle 1 \rangle$. Contrast: $\overline{\mathbb{C}P^2} \# \mathbb{C}P^2$ has form $\langle 1 \rangle \oplus \langle -1 \rangle$.
